findings.title: No cookie consent banner or mechanism
findings.description: The application uses Plausible Analytics without explicit opt-in consent. GDPR requires freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent for non-essential cookies and analytics. The script is loaded unconditionally on every page visit, regardless of user consent status.
findings.evidence: <script async src="https://analytics.codyjo.com/js/pa-h94dmAMXaqc3wcP-F6Elo.js"></script> loaded unconditionally; no consent banner present
findings.fix: 1) Integrate a GDPR-compliant cookie consent library (Cookiebot, OneTrust, tarteaucitron). 2) Implement a consent banner that blocks non-essential scripts until user opts in. 3) Display granular consent choices. 4) Store consent preferences securely. 5) Provide easy withdrawal mechanism.

findings.title: No gallery email allowlist — unauthenticated access to galleries
findings.description: The auth_view handler contains a TODO comment explicitly noting: 'Currently any syntactically valid email grants a JWT for any gallery, which is effectively unauthenticated access.' Any user can enter any email and receive a valid JWT to browse any gallery.
findings.evidence: TODO (CLIENT-03): Currently any syntactically valid email grants a JWT for any gallery, which is effectively unauthenticated access.
findings.fix: 1) Add 'allowed_emails' or 'allowed_domains' field to each gallery. 2) Validate submitted email against allowlist before issuing JWT. 3) For public galleries, explicitly set 'is_public' flag. 4) Log access attempts and alert on suspicious patterns.

findings.title: Privacy policy incomplete — missing key GDPR disclosures
findings.description: Privacy policy lacks GDPR-required disclosures: DPO contact, explicit retention periods, international transfer disclosure, legal basis per purpose, automated decision-making details, and data subject rights information.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lacks DPO contact, retention detail, transfer disclosure, legal basis, and data subject rights information.
findings.fix: 1) Add 'Retention' section with specific periods for each data type. 2) Add 'Legal Basis' section. 3) Add 'Contact for data subject requests' email. 4) Add 'Data Protection Officer' section. 5) Disclose AWS data processing and transfers. 6) Add 'Data Subject Rights' section.

findings.title: Data subject access/export endpoints are admin-only, not user-accessible
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 15, 20 (Right of Access & Data Portability)
findings.description: GDPR endpoints (/admin/gdpr/search, /admin/gdpr/erase) are super-admin protected. Photographers have no self-service mechanism to comply with data subject access requests. This creates operational friction and potential GDPR compliance issues.
findings.evidence: GDPR endpoints require X-Admin-Key authentication. No user-facing data export endpoints exist.
findings.fix: 1) Create user-accessible /account/data-export endpoint. 2) Implement 'My Data' dashboard. 3) Add 'Delete My Account' option. 4) Document SAR fulfillment procedures. 5) Set up privacy-requests@analogifystudio.com email.

findings.title: Gallery access control missing privacy-by-design elements
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 25 (Data Protection by Design and Default)
findings.description: Galleries are created without explicit privacy settings. No default 'private' mode requiring photographers to opt-in to public access. No consent withdrawal mechanism for clients.
findings.evidence: Gallery creation lacks privacy_level, allowed_recipients, or public/private flag.
findings.fix: 1) Add 'privacy_level' field (private/link-only/public). 2) Default galleries to 'private' mode. 3) Allow clients to revoke access. 4) Implement consent withdrawal.

findings.title: No public Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with AWS
findings.description: Application uses AWS services without publicizing the Data Processing Agreement terms. Privacy policy does not link AWS DPA or explain processor role.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions AWS generically but does not link DPA or explain processor status.
findings.fix: 1) Link AWS Data Processing Addendum in privacy policy. 2) Clarify photographer as controller, Analogify/AWS as processor. 3) Disclose AWS region(s). 4) Explain transfer mechanism (SCCs, adequacy decision).

findings.title: Vague data retention policy; no documented retention schedule
findings.description: Privacy policy states 'about 30 days' for comments but no formal Data Retention Schedule is published. Different tables have different TTLs not communicated to users.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy: 'about 30 days' for comments; Terraform shows TTLs (access_log 1 year, jobs 90 days) not published to users.
findings.fix: 1) Create 'Data Retention Schedule' table in privacy policy. 2) List each data type, retention period, deletion method. 3) Add section 'When is my data deleted?' explaining TTL and procedures.

findings.title: No centralized logging of authentication/authorization events
findings.description: Lambda handlers log to CloudWatch but no centralized audit trail or SIEM integration. No monitoring/alerting for security events.
findings.evidence: Logging to CloudWatch only; no centralized monitoring, alerting, or audit trail aggregation.
findings.fix: 1) Set up CloudWatch Insights queries for auth failures. 2) Create alarms for suspicious patterns. 3) Integrate with Security Hub or SIEM. 4) Document incident response procedures.

findings.title: No public incident response or breach notification contact
findings.description: Privacy policy does not provide contact method for reporting security incidents. No security.txt file for researchers to report vulnerabilities.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists general contact (cody@codyjo.com) but not specifically for security incidents.
findings.fix: 1) Create /.well-known/security.txt per RFC 9110. 2) Add to privacy: 'Report vulnerabilities to security@analogifystudio.com'. 3) Document vulnerability disclosure policy. 4) Set up autoresponder.

findings.title: No published DPO contact information
findings.description: Privacy policy does not mention DPO status or contact. If DPO is appointed, contact must be published per GDPR.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists contact as 'cody@codyjo.com' without specifying DPO status.
findings.fix: 1) Add: 'Analogify does not have a mandatory DPO' or 'DPO contact: dpo@analogifystudio.com'. 2) Make DPO contact accessible in footer. 3) Review if DPO appointment becomes required with scaling.

findings.title: Missing Privacy Policy Documentation and Transparency
findings.description: The auth-service handles substantial personal data (email, name, password hashes, encryption keys) but there is no reference to a privacy policy document, no disclosure of data purposes, recipients, or retention periods. While the code mentions 'privacyPolicyVersion' in registration, there is no actual policy linked or enforced via the API.
findings.evidence: Line 356 references 'privacyPolicyVersion: "2026-03"' but no privacy policy URL or endpoint exists. Users cannot access transparent information about how their data is processed.
findings.fix: 1) Create and publish a comprehensive privacy policy covering: data controller/DPO contact, purposes of processing, categories of data, recipients, retention periods, data subject rights, GDPR legal basis. 2) Add a /api/auth/privacy-policy endpoint that returns the current policy version and URL. 3) Maintain policy version history in documentation with changelog. 4) Link privacy policy on client-side registration pages (handled by consuming apps). 5) Document GDPR legal basis (likely Article 6(1)(a) - consent, or Article 6(1)(b) - contract).

findings.title: No Data Portability / Data Export Endpoint
findings.description: Users have a right to receive their personal data in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format (JSON, CSV, XML). The auth-service provides no endpoint to export user data, violating the right to data portability.
findings.evidence: Reviewed all API endpoints (lines 269-292). There is no /api/auth/export-data or similar endpoint. Users cannot exercise their right to data portability.
findings.fix: 1) Add a new endpoint /api/auth/data-export (GET, requires auth token) that returns user profile, consent records, and activity metadata in JSON format. 2) Include email, name, userId, emailVerified, createdAt, consentedAt, passwordChangedAt, and any app-specific data linked via userId. 3) Return data within 30 days of request. 4) Implement audit logging of data export requests. 5) Consider rate limiting to prevent data scraping.

findings.title: No Documented Data Retention Policy
findings.description: GDPR requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary. There is no documented retention policy for user profiles, verification codes, reset codes, or other personal data. Data appears to be retained indefinitely unless explicitly deleted.
findings.evidence: User profiles are stored with no TTL. Verification codes have 24-hour TTL (line 370: expiresAt: now + 86400), reset codes have 15-minute TTL (line 508: expiresAt: now + 900), but user profile data has no automatic expiration. Manual deletion via /api/account DELETE endpoint is the only removal mechanism.
findings.fix: 1) Define and document a data retention policy: user profiles (retained for account lifetime + 90 days after deletion for legal holds), verification codes (24 hours, already implemented), reset codes (15 minutes, already implemented), admin audit logs (2 years). 2) Implement automatic TTL deletion in DynamoDB for infrequently accessed accounts (e.g., accounts inactive for 3+ years) after user notification. 3) Add a retention_policy.md file documenting how long each data type is kept and why. 4) Provide a /api/auth/account/export endpoint for users to download their data before automatic deletion.

findings.title: No DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) Documentation
findings.description: The auth-service processes personal data related to authentication, encryption, and admin management. This system should have a DPIA to evaluate compliance risks and mitigations. No DPIA documentation is present in the codebase.
findings.evidence: No DPIA documentation found in the repository. The system processes passwords, encryption keys, and user identifiers—all sensitive data that warrants risk assessment.
findings.fix: 1) Conduct a DPIA covering: data flows, third-party recipients (AWS, Resend), security controls, risk assessments, mitigation measures. 2) Document high-risk areas: JWT secret storage in Secrets Manager, encryption key management, admin access to user data, email transmission via Resend. 3) Create a dpia.md file in the repository documenting the assessment, risks identified, and mitigations. 4) Review annually or when significant changes are made (new processors, new data types, new purposes).

findings.title: No Documented Data Processing Agreements (DPAs)
findings.description: The auth-service uses third-party processors (AWS DynamoDB, AWS Secrets Manager, Resend email service). GDPR requires Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) or Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) with all processors. No DPA documentation is present.
findings.evidence: Repository does not document DPAs with AWS or Resend. Terraform configuration shows use of these services, but no SCCs or DPA documentation is included.
findings.fix: 1) Verify that AWS has executed a Data Processing Addendum (DPA) as part of the AWS Business Associate Agreement (BAA) or Data Processing Agreement. 2) Obtain a DPA from Resend or verify SCCs are in place for email processing. 3) Create a dpa-compliance.md file documenting: which processors are used, what data they process, DPA/SCC dates, and review schedule. 4) Maintain an inventory of sub-processors (e.g., CDN, logging) and ensure consent for sub-processor changes is documented.

findings.title: Consent Collection Lacks Granularity and Transparency
findings.description: The registration endpoint requires a 'consent' boolean parameter but does not clarify what the user is consenting to or provide granular consent options (e.g., separate consent for email marketing, analytics, etc.). The consent mechanism is a binary checkbox without layered notices.
findings.evidence: Line 305: 'if (!consent) return res(400, { error: "Consent to privacy policy is required" }); '. The code does not specify what consent entails, and there is no mechanism for withdrawing consent granularly.
findings.fix: 1) Expand consent collection to include granular options: 'I consent to account creation and authentication' (essential), 'I consent to marketing emails' (optional), 'I consent to analytics' (optional). 2) Add a /api/auth/consent endpoint to withdraw or modify consent at any time. 3) Store consent records with: timestamp, version of policy/terms, specific consents given (as a JSON object), IP address, and user agent. 4) Implement a preference center where users can manage their consent selections. 5) Ensure privacy policy is presented during registration with plain language explaining data use.

findings.title: No Documented Breach Notification Process
findings.description: GDPR requires notification of data breaches to the supervisory authority within 72 hours and to affected individuals. There is no documented incident response plan, breach notification templates, or mechanisms for detecting and reporting breaches.
findings.evidence: No incident response documentation, breach notification templates, or alerting configuration found. The CLAUDE.md mentions CloudWatch logs (/codebuild/auth-service) but no breach detection or alerting strategy.
findings.fix: 1) Create an incident-response.md file documenting: breach definitions, detection procedures, notification templates for supervisory authorities and users, escalation procedures. 2) Set up CloudWatch alarms for: failed authentication attempts (pattern: >10 in 1 minute), unauthorized DynamoDB queries, Secrets Manager access failures, Lambda errors. 3) Document a 72-hour breach notification timeline and designate responsible parties. 4) Implement a audit log for all data access events with timestamps, user IDs, and actions. 5) Schedule quarterly breach response drills.

findings.title: Excessive Data Collection in Admin Operations
findings.description: The admin list-users endpoint returns full user profiles including sensitive fields. The endpoint scans up to 10 pages of the database without respecting data minimization principles. Admin users could potentially enumerate all user data.
findings.evidence: Lines 108-154: The handleListUsers function scans PROFILE items across up to 10 database pages (MAX_SCAN_PAGES=10). While it returns limited fields to admins, the scan itself touches all profile data in the database.
findings.fix: 1) Limit the number of scan pages to 3-5 instead of 10 to reduce database load and data exposure. 2) Consider indexing by createdAt to support pagination by date rather than full table scans. 3) Filter user list responses to return only: userId, email, name, emailVerified, disabled (omit sensitive fields). 4) Implement audit logging for admin list operations with query parameters. 5) Document why each field is returned to admins.

findings.title: Missing Security Headers on API Responses
findings.description: API responses lack critical security headers (Content-Security-Policy, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, Cache-Control). These headers prevent common web vulnerabilities like XSS, clickjacking, MIME sniffing, and cache poisoning.
findings.evidence: Lines 116-122: The corsHeaders function returns only CORS headers and Content-Type. No security headers are included in any response. Example missing headers: 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000', 'X-Frame-Options: DENY', 'Content-Security-Policy: default-src \'self\''.
findings.fix: 1) Add security headers to the res() function: 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains' (enforce HTTPS for 1 year). 2) Add 'X-Frame-Options: DENY' to prevent clickjacking. 3) Add 'X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff' to prevent MIME sniffing. 4) Add 'Content-Security-Policy: default-src \'self\'; script-src \'none\'; object-src \'none\''. 5) Add 'Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin'. 6) Add 'Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()'. 7) For sensitive endpoints, add 'Cache-Control: private, no-store' to prevent caching of auth tokens.

findings.title: Incomplete Audit Logging for Sensitive Operations
findings.description: While the code logs authentication and admin events to console (JSON format), there is no persistent, immutable audit trail. Logs are sent to CloudWatch but not configured for immutability or long-term retention. Some sensitive operations (e.g., encryption key changes) lack detailed audit context.
findings.evidence: Lines 152, 172, 208, 257, 388, 417, 441, 525, 589, 644, 700, 737, 860, 887, 928: Audit logs are written to console.log(JSON.stringify(...)). There is no indication these logs are configured for immutability, long-term retention (e.g., 2 years), or forwarded to a centralized logging system.
findings.fix: 1) Configure CloudWatch Logs retention policy to at least 2 years for all /codebuild/auth-service logs. 2) Enable CloudWatch Logs encryption at rest. 3) Consider forwarding logs to a centralized system (e.g., AWS CloudTrail, Splunk, Datadog) for immutability and analysis. 4) Add audit logging context to password change operations (line 727): log 'ip', 'timestamp', 'action', 'affected_fields' (e.g., 'password_hash_changed'). 5) Log all admin actions with admin email, timestamp, target user, and action taken. 6) Implement log integrity checks (e.g., hash verification) to detect tampering.

findings.title: JWT Tokens Not Invalidated on Password Change
findings.description: When a user changes their password, existing JWT tokens remain valid until expiry (30 days). An attacker with a stolen token can continue accessing the account even after the password is changed. While passwordChangedAt invalidation is checked on token use, this is insufficient for true token invalidation.
findings.evidence: Lines 173-176: Token validity is checked against passwordChangedAt, which is a mitigation but not true invalidation. A token issued before a password change will fail validation (token.iat < passwordChangedAt), but this relies on the server always checking this condition. Tokens are not explicitly revoked.
findings.fix: 1) Implement a token revocation list (revocation store in DynamoDB) where invalidated token IDs are tracked. 2) Add a 'jti' (JWT ID) claim to tokens that uniquely identifies each token. 3) When changing password (line 727), also: increment a 'tokenVersion' field on the user profile, check this version on token validation. 4) On logout (if implemented), delete the token from the revocation list or increment tokenVersion. 5) Alternatively, reduce JWT expiry from 30 days to 24 hours and require refresh tokens (which can be revoked immediately). 6) Document this strategy in security.md.

findings.title: No Explicit API Rate Limiting on All Endpoints
findings.description: While some endpoints have rate limiting (login: 5 attempts per 15 min, register: 3 per hour, password change: 5 per 15 min), other sensitive endpoints like /api/auth/password (PUT), /api/auth/email (PUT), and admin endpoints lack comprehensive rate limiting.
findings.evidence: Line 287: handleChangePassword has rate limiting (line 711). Lines 742-808: handleChangeEmail does NOT have rate limiting. Admin endpoints (lines 91-97) have no rate limiting outside of implicit DynamoDB limits.
findings.fix: 1) Add rate limiting to /api/auth/email (PUT): 3 attempts per hour per user. 2) Add rate limiting to /api/auth/name (PUT): 10 attempts per hour per user. 3) Add rate limiting to admin endpoints: /api/admin/users (GET): 100 per minute per admin, /api/admin/users/detail (GET): 1000 per minute per admin, other admin actions: 10 per minute per admin. 4) Use consistent rate limit keys (userId or admin email) across all endpoints. 5) Return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header.

findings.title: Weak Password Policy - No Complexity Requirements
findings.description: The auth-service enforces a minimum password length of 10 characters but does not require password complexity (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters). This allows weak passwords like '1111111111' or 'aaaaaaaaaa'.
findings.evidence: Lines 307, 533, 708: Password validation checks only 'password.length < 10'. No regex or complexity validation is performed. A user could register with password '0000000000' and it would pass validation.
findings.fix: 1) Update password validation regex to require: minimum 12 characters (or keep 10 with complexity), at least one uppercase letter, at least one lowercase letter, at least one digit, at least one special character (!@#$%^&*). 2) Check passwords against a breached password database (e.g., HaveIBeenPwned API) to prevent users from choosing commonly breached passwords. 3) Document password policy in privacy policy / terms of service. 4) Return helpful error messages: 'Password must contain uppercase, lowercase, digit, and special character' instead of generic 'password too weak'.

findings.title: No Documented Secret Rotation Policy for JWT Secret and API Keys
findings.description: The JWT secret and Resend API key are stored in AWS Secrets Manager with no documented rotation schedule. There is no procedure for rotating these secrets or preventing secret leakage impact.
findings.evidence: Lines 122-135 (terraform/lambda.tf): JWT secret is created and initially set but no rotation policy or versioning strategy is documented. No indication of how to rotate the secret or invalidate old tokens.
findings.fix: 1) Document a secret rotation policy in a security-operations.md file: JWT secret rotated every 90 days, Resend API key rotated annually or immediately if suspected compromise. 2) Implement secret versioning in Secrets Manager (already supported). 3) Update Lambda environment to fetch the latest secret version (currently always fetches latest, which is good). 4) Create a reminder/notification for quarterly secret rotation. 5) Test secret rotation in a staging environment before production rollout. 6) Document a compromise response plan: rotate JWT secret, force re-authentication of all active users (by invalidating tokens), rotate Resend API key.

findings.title: API key stored in plaintext in configuration file
findings.description: The Back Office API key is stored as plaintext in api-config.yaml. While the file may be in .gitignore, it is still accessible on the filesystem to any user with file access. A compromised config file exposes the API key, allowing unauthorized access to all Back Office API endpoints including scan triggering and agent control.
findings.evidence: config file contains api_key field with plaintext string value
findings.fix: Move API key to environment variables. Update config.py to load from: os.getenv('BACKOFFICE_API_KEY', '')

findings.title: API key stored in plaintext in configuration file
findings.description: The Back Office API key is stored as plaintext in backoffice.example.yaml. While the file may be in .gitignore, it is still accessible on the filesystem to any user with file access. A compromised config file exposes the API key, allowing unauthorized access to all Back Office API endpoints including scan triggering and agent control.
findings.evidence: config file contains api_key field with plaintext string value
findings.fix: Move API key to environment variables. Update config.py to load from: os.getenv('BACKOFFICE_API_KEY', '')

findings.title: API key stored in plaintext in configuration file
findings.description: The Back Office API key is stored as plaintext in backoffice.yaml. While the file may be in .gitignore, it is still accessible on the filesystem to any user with file access. A compromised config file exposes the API key, allowing unauthorized access to all Back Office API endpoints including scan triggering and agent control.
findings.evidence: config file contains api_key field with plaintext string value
findings.fix: Move API key to environment variables. Update config.py to load from: os.getenv('BACKOFFICE_API_KEY', '')

findings.title: API server does not enforce TLS/HTTPS for sensitive data transmission
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.24 (Encryption in Transit), A.5.17 (Access Control)
findings.description: The Back Office API server can bind to 0.0.0.0 and has no mandatory TLS enforcement. API authentication uses HMAC with X-API-Key header transmitted in plaintext HTTP, allowing network eavesdropping and credential capture.
findings.evidence: No ssl.SSLContext usage found; api_server binds to 0.0.0.0 by default
findings.fix: Implement TLS using Python's ssl module. Generate self-signed certificates or use reverse proxy (nginx). Restrict bind address to 127.0.0.1 by default.

findings.title: No API key rotation mechanism or expiry policy
findings.description: API keys appear to be long-lived static credentials with no automatic expiry or rotation. Keys lack metadata (created_at, expires_at) and there is no mechanism to deprecate or invalidate old keys.
findings.evidence: api_key is a plain string with no ttl, expiry_date, or rotation_date field
findings.fix: Implement key versioning with metadata. Add key expiry (90 days). Implement rotation command and document policy.

findings.title: File permissions not explicitly set on created findings files
findings.description: Results files are created without explicit permission settings. Default umask might create world-readable files containing audit findings and system information.
findings.evidence: json.dump() without explicit mode/chmod after file creation
findings.fix: After creating results files, set restrictive permissions: os.chmod(path, 0o600) or 0o640 for group read

findings.title: No documentation of Data Processing Agreements with third-party processors
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.9 (Systems access control), Industry practice
findings.description: Back-Office uses Anthropic (Claude API) and AWS services as data processors. There is no documented evidence of Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) or clauses restricting how these processors handle findings data.
findings.evidence: No DPA or processor contract documentation found
findings.fix: Obtain/verify Data Processing Addendum from Anthropic and AWS. Document processor details and retention policies in README or COMPLIANCE.md

findings.title: No documented backup and disaster recovery procedures for audit data
findings.description: Back-Office stores persistent audit findings in backlog.json and dashboard data in dashboard/ directory. There is no documented backup strategy, disaster recovery procedures, or RTO/RPO targets for these critical audit records.
findings.evidence: No backup procedures documented; no RTO/RPO targets specified
findings.fix: Document backup strategy: daily backups of backlog.json to S3 with versioning enabled. Define RTO (1 day) and RPO (24 hours).

findings.title: No documented data retention policy for audit findings and backlog
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.13 (Backup), Industry Standard Data Governance
findings.description: Back-Office stores audit findings indefinitely in backlog.json with no documented retention period or purging strategy. Historical findings are never deleted, creating unbounded storage growth and retention of potentially outdated information.
findings.evidence: No code or documentation specifying TTL or deletion policy for old findings
findings.fix: Define retention policy: keep last 12 months of findings, archive older findings monthly. Implement pruning in update_score_history().

findings.title: No age verification gate despite stated 16+ age requirement
findings.regulation: Utah S.B. 152, Texas HB 1181, Virginia SB 1515, Arkansas SB 396, California AB 2273
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'account registration is intended only for people who are at least 16 years old,' but the registration flow contains no age verification, date-of-birth collection, or age gate. US state laws (Utah S.B. 152, Texas HB 1181, Virginia SB 1515, Arkansas SB 396) and California AADC (AB 2273) require age verification for online platforms accessible to minors.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy line 81 states age requirement, but lambda/api/index.mjs#359-440 (handleRegister) accepts any email/password/name without age verification. No date-of-birth field or third-party age verification integration.
findings.fix: Implement age verification gate: (1) Add date-of-birth field to registration form. (2) Validate server-side to confirm user is 16+ (preferably 18+). (3) Consider third-party age verification (Yoti, Jumio, Veriff). (4) Store verification status in DynamoDB. (5) Update privacy policy with verification mechanism. (6) Implement jurisdiction-specific age rules for US users.

findings.title: No explicit data processing consent collection
findings.description: No evidence of explicit consent collection for data processing. Privacy policy exists but lacks: (1) interactive consent banner/modal at registration; (2) granular consent choices (essential vs. analytics vs. marketing); (3) consent confirmation records with timestamp and version; (4) consent withdrawal mechanism. GDPR Art. 6 requires lawful basis for processing; Art. 7 mandates consent be freely given, specific, informed, unambiguous.
findings.evidence: Registration form (register/page.tsx#51-82) collects name, email, password without consent checkboxes or privacy policy acknowledgment. No consent records in DynamoDB schema.
findings.fix: Implement consent collection: (1) Add consent banner/modal to registration with clear descriptions. (2) Require explicit checkboxes (not pre-checked) for each optional consent. (3) Store consent records in DynamoDB with userId, date, version, scopes. (4) Add preference center for consent management. (5) Log consent changes for audit.

findings.title: No Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with third parties
findings.description: Personal data transferred to multiple third parties without documented DPAs: (1) Anthropic (Claude API for tutoring) - lambda/tutor/index.mjs sends user questions to Claude. (2) Resend (email) - lambda/api/index.mjs#204-225 sends user emails to Resend API. (3) Plausible (analytics) - src/app/layout.tsx includes Plausible script. (4) AWS (S3, DynamoDB) - US-based infrastructure. Privacy policy mentions transfers vaguely but lacks: (a) confirmation of SCCs/BCRs; (b) transparency about transfer safeguards; (c) complete recipient list.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy (lines 56-59) is generic: 'CertStudy is hosted primarily on Amazon Web Services in the United States. AI tutor responses use Anthropic...' Lacks specificity about SCCs, DPAs, or complete recipient list. Plausible analytics not mentioned.
findings.fix: Establish DPAs and document safeguards: (1) Obtain signed Data Processing Agreements from Anthropic, Resend, Plausible, AWS. (2) Confirm SCCs in place with each vendor. (3) Update privacy policy: explicitly list recipients (Anthropic, Resend, Plausible, AWS) with locations and roles. (4) Add section 'International Data Transfers' explaining SCCs/safeguards. (5) For EU users, display transfer notice or offer EU-only option. (6) Document transfer mechanism in DPIA.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) documented
findings.description: CertStudy processes personal data through high-risk activities requiring DPIA per GDPR Art. 35: (1) Large-scale processing of special categories (educational records, learning patterns). (2) Systematic monitoring (quiz results, flashcard performance tracking). (3) International transfer to US. (4) AI/automated decision-making (Claude API tutoring). (5) Long-term educational record storage. No DPIA documentation found.
findings.evidence: No DPIA documentation in repo. Privacy policy does not reference DPIA. Terraform config contains no DPIA references.
findings.fix: Commission or perform DPIA: (1) Document processing: purpose, data types, recipients, retention, technical/organizational measures. (2) Assess risks: unauthorized access, data breaches, profiling abuse, AI impact. (3) Identify mitigations: encryption, access controls, audit logs, data minimization. (4) Document findings in DPIA report. (5) Share with DPO if applicable. (6) Update annually.

findings.title: Limited right to restrict processing and right to object mechanisms
findings.description: While account deletion and data export are implemented, mechanisms for right to restrict processing (Art. 18) and right to object (Art. 21) are missing. Privacy policy mentions rights generically but lacks specific implementation. Users cannot: (1) restrict processing without deletion; (2) opt out of analytics/profiling; (3) object to marketing or automated decision-making.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy (lines 75-77) mentions rights but only DELETE /api/account and GET /api/export are implemented. No endpoints for restricting processing or objecting to non-essential processing.
findings.fix: Add data subject rights endpoints: (1) PATCH /api/account/processing to restrict processing. (2) PATCH /api/account/analytics-consent to opt out of analytics. (3) Add settings page for data rights management. (4) Implement correction workflow for inaccurate data. (5) Document prominently in privacy policy with direct links.

findings.title: Excessive data collection in tutor/AI interactions
findings.description: Tutor Lambda (lambda/tutor/index.mjs#315-410) stores full conversation history without clear data minimization. Each session records: full user messages, full AI responses, learning context. While conversation history is necessary for feature functionality, data is not minimized and retention periods are unclear.
findings.evidence: Tutor Lambda (line 359-410) stores full messages and responses in DynamoDB. No redaction, summarization, or compression. No documented retention period beyond generic TTL.
findings.fix: Implement data minimization: (1) Store only essential metadata (userId, sessionId, trackId, timestamp, duration). (2) Do NOT store full messages by default; optionally store with 7-day TTL. (3) Allow users to opt-in to history retention. (4) Implement 90-day retention policy with auto-deletion. (5) Provide UI to view/delete sessions. (6) Update privacy policy with tutor data retention policy.

findings.title: Limited logging of security-relevant events
findings.description: API Lambda includes minimal logging (logAuthEvent function). Missing: (1) failed authentication attempts; (2) authorization failures; (3) modification events; (4) data export/deletion details; (5) tutor session events; (6) error tracking; (7) IP/user-agent for forensics. ISO 27001 requires comprehensive audit logging with: event type, timestamp, actor, action, object, outcome, details.
findings.evidence: Logging function (line 235-237) is minimal: only console.log. Auth events logged but not failed attempts, authorization failures, or modifications.
findings.fix: Implement comprehensive audit logging: (1) Create auditLog() function capturing: timestamp, event type, userId, sourceIp, userAgent, action, object, outcome, details. (2) Log authentication (success/failure), authorization, data ops, errors, rate limits. (3) Store logs in CloudWatch with 30+ day retention. (4) Implement CloudWatch rules to alert on suspicious patterns. (5) Provide audit log download via API (restricted to admin).

findings.title: Sensitive data stored unencrypted in localStorage
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 25 (Privacy by Design and Default)
findings.description: Client-side code stores sensitive data in localStorage without encryption: (1) 'cert_token' (JWT cleartext); (2) 'cert_user' (profile cleartext); (3) 'cert_preferences' (cleartext); (4) 'cert_enc_cache' (encryption keys). If device is compromised, attackers can extract tokens and PII. GDPR Art. 25 requires privacy by design and high-privacy defaults.
findings.evidence: Storage constants (lines 28-40) store data in localStorage without encryption. Token retrieval (auth-context.tsx line 25): localStorage.getItem(TOKEN_KEY) — no decryption.
findings.fix: Encrypt sensitive localStorage data: (1) Derive encryption key from password (PBKDF2). (2) Use AES-256-GCM to encrypt 'cert_token', 'cert_user', 'cert_enc_cache'. (3) Prompt for password unlock on page load. (4) Use sessionStorage (expires on tab close) for tokens instead of localStorage. (5) Implement automatic logout on inactivity (30 minutes).

findings.title: Privacy policy incomplete: missing AI profiling, data accuracy, analytics disclosure
findings.description: Privacy policy lacks: (1) automated decision-making/profiling disclosure; (2) data accuracy mechanisms; (3) exact retention periods (vague 'about 30 days'); (4) right to lodge complaint with supervisory authority; (5) complete recipient list (Plausible, Resend omitted); (6) analytics disclosure.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy (lines 62-64) vaguely mentions 'AI boundaries' but not profiling. No mention of Plausible analytics. No supervisory authority complaint right. Retention periods vague ('about 30 days').
findings.fix: Expand privacy policy: (1) Add 'Automated Processing': disclose Claude AI profiling for tutoring recommendations. (2) Add 'Data Accuracy' section: users can flag inaccurate data; correction within 7 days. (3) Specify exact retention: 'Operational logs: 30 days. Security logs: 90 days. Tutor sessions: 180 days.' (4) Add complaint right: 'Lodge complaint with supervisory authority [link].' (5) Complete recipient list: 'AWS, Anthropic, Resend, Plausible.' (6) Add analytics disclosure: 'Plausible (EU-based, no cookies, GDPR-compliant).' (7) Review annually.

findings.title: No multi-factor authentication (MFA) support
findings.description: Authentication uses password-only (single-factor). No MFA/2FA, TOTP, SMS OTP, recovery codes, or backup methods. While passwords use strong PBKDF2-SHA512, single-factor is insufficient for sensitive educational/PII data. ISO 27001 recommends MFA for sensitive systems.
findings.evidence: Login handler (line 442-475) accepts only email/password. No MFA. Registration does not set up MFA. No MFA fields in DynamoDB or API endpoints.
findings.fix: Implement optional/mandatory MFA: (1) Add TOTP support (speakeasy library). (2) On login, prompt for TOTP if MFA enabled. (3) In settings: enable/disable MFA, generate recovery codes. (4) Add endpoints: POST /api/auth/mfa/setup, POST /api/auth/mfa/verify, POST /api/auth/mfa/disable. (5) Store TOTP secret (encrypted), MFA flag, recovery codes in DynamoDB. (6) Update UI: settings page MFA management.

findings.title: No age-appropriate design features for minor users
findings.regulation: California AB 2273, UK Online Safety Act 2023, Ofcom Children's Code
findings.description: Privacy policy claims 'not directed to children under 13' and '16+ registration', yet design lacks age-appropriate protections: (1) no content filters/parental controls; (2) no default high-privacy for minors; (3) no profiling/data-sharing restrictions; (4) no dark pattern protections; (5) no age-appropriate privacy info. California AB 2273 (AADC), UK OSA 2023, Ofcom Children's Code require age-appropriate design.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy claims age requirements (line 80-82) but no code prevents child accounts, no content filters, no minor-specific privacy settings.
findings.fix: Implement age-appropriate design (requires architectural changes): (1) Age verification (see COMP-001). (2) For minors: high-privacy defaults (opt-in analytics), parental controls, study limits, no dark patterns. (3) For adults (18+): current design. (4) Restrict: profiling, third-party sharing for minors. (5) Parental controls: optional setup if parent creates child account. (6) No infinite scroll, autoplay, addictive design for minors.

findings.title: Verbose error messages may leak sensitive information
findings.description: API error messages leak information enabling account enumeration, validation attacks: (1) 'Email not found vs. password incorrect' distinction; (2) 'Password must be at least 10 characters' reveals policy; (3) 'Invalid email format' validates email externally. Attackers can enumerate accounts, reverse-engineer API constraints.
findings.evidence: Error messages: line 362 'Password must be at least 10 characters'; line 387 generic response; line 815 'Password is incorrect'; line 367 'Invalid email format'.
findings.fix: Use generic error messages: (1) Auth failures: 'Invalid email or password' (don't distinguish user existence). (2) Validation errors: 'Invalid request' (don't reveal specific failures to unauthenticated users). (3) Log detailed errors server-side. (4) Detailed errors only to authenticated users. (5) Example: delete account should return 'Authentication failed' not 'Password is incorrect'.

findings.title: Plausible analytics not explicitly disclosed or consented to
findings.description: Plausible Analytics included in CSP and layout but not disclosed in privacy policy. While Plausible is GDPR-compliant (no cookies), GDPR Art. 7 requires clear consent, Art. 21 allows opt-out. Privacy policy should explicitly disclose analytics.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy (line 36-46) does not mention Plausible. Layout.tsx includes Plausible script but no consent mechanism.
findings.fix: Disclose analytics: (1) Add to privacy policy: 'We use Plausible Analytics (GDPR-compliant, no cookies) to understand site usage.' (2) Add opt-out: 'Opt out of analytics [link].' (3) Alternatively, implement consent banner with 'Allow analytics?' option. (4) Update privacy policy with opt-out method.

findings.title: No data subject rights mechanisms (access, deletion, portability)
findings.description: The codyjo.com website collects personal data through a contact form (name, email, message) and server logs (IP, user agent), but provides no mechanisms for users to exercise their fundamental GDPR rights: access to personal data (Art. 15), deletion of personal data (Art. 17), or data portability (Art. 20). These are mandatory rights in GDPR compliance. The privacy policy acknowledges these rights exist but provides no process to fulfill them.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy line 116-121 states 'You can request access to, correction of, or deletion of personal data held through this site' but no mechanism (email, form, portal) is provided. Contact form only collects data, does not provide data export or deletion.
findings.fix: 1. Create a 'Download My Data' feature that exports all collected personal data (contact form submissions) as JSON/CSV. 2. Create an 'Account Deletion Request' mechanism (email-based workflow to process erasure). 3. Update privacy policy with clear contact information and process for exercising rights. 4. Implement backend data export/deletion endpoint accessible via authenticated API or email-based workflow. 5. Document data retention periods and implement automatic deletion after retention window.

findings.title: No data retention policy documented or implemented
findings.description: The privacy policy does not specify how long personal data collected through the contact form is retained. GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) requires storage limitation: personal data must be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than necessary for the purposes for which the data are processed. There is no visible TTL, archive, or deletion mechanism for contact form data.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy does not mention data retention periods. Contact form sends data to AWS Lambda API (https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact) with no visible retention or deletion configuration.
findings.fix: 1. Define data retention policy: e.g., 'Contact form submissions are retained for 12 months for correspondence purposes, then automatically deleted.' 2. Implement TTL/expiry on contact data in backend storage (DynamoDB, S3, etc.). 3. Add explicit retention period to privacy policy (Section 2 or new section). 4. Document and test automatic deletion process (Lambda scheduled event, S3 lifecycle policy, etc.).

findings.title: Consent mechanism lacks granularity and clear lawful basis
findings.description: The contact form collects a privacy consent checkbox, but the consent is bundled: users must agree to 'processing of data as described in the Privacy Policy' to submit the form. The privacy policy does not distinguish between different processing purposes (contact response vs. analytics vs. server logs) or provide granular consent options. Users cannot selectively consent to contact communication while opting out of analytics, for example.
findings.evidence: Consent checkbox (line 306-308) states: 'I agree to the processing of my data as described in the Privacy Policy.' No granular choices (analytics, marketing, contact response) are offered. Privacy policy (src/pages/privacy.astro) mentions Plausible analytics separately but does not offer opt-in/opt-out mechanism.
findings.fix: 1. Offer granular consent checkboxes separate from form submission: 'I agree to be contacted about my inquiry' (required to submit) vs. 'I consent to analytics' (optional). 2. Allow form submission without analytics consent. 3. Implement cookie consent banner or preference center to manage Plausible analytics separately from contact form consent. 4. Update privacy policy to clearly identify lawful basis for each processing purpose (consent for contact, legitimate interest for security logs, etc.).

findings.title: Privacy policy missing some required GDPR disclosures
findings.description: The privacy policy is comprehensive but lacks a few mandatory GDPR disclosures: (1) whether provision of personal data is a statutory or contractual requirement, (2) whether the service involves automated decision-making or profiling with legal/significant effects, (3) DPO contact information (if applicable), and (4) right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy sections cover lawful basis, third parties, and rights, but do not explicitly state: whether providing data is optional or required (the form implies it's required for contact), whether any automated decisions affect the user, who the Data Protection Officer is (if one exists), or which supervisory authority can be contacted (e.g., 'you can contact the [relevant EU] data protection authority').
findings.fix: 1. Add statement: 'You may voluntarily provide contact information to send an inquiry. Providing this information is optional; however, without it we cannot respond to your message.' 2. Add: 'We do not use automated decision-making or profiling.' (if true) or describe any such processing. 3. Add DPO contact info if applicable, or state 'No Data Protection Officer is designated for this site.' 4. Add: 'You have the right to lodge a complaint with your national data protection authority' and link to relevant authority (e.g., EDPB or UK ICO).

findings.title: No visible logging or audit trail configuration for contact form data
findings.description: The contact form sends data to an AWS Lambda API endpoint, but there is no visible logging configuration (CloudWatch logs, audit trails, or event logging) for contact form submissions. Without audit logging, it is impossible to detect unauthorized access, deletions, or modifications to personal data in compliance with ISO 27001 A.8.15 and GDPR Art. 32.
findings.evidence: Contact form submits to https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact (line 343) with no visible logging, error tracking, or audit trail. The form client-side code does not reference any logging; backend Lambda is not visible in codebase.
findings.fix: 1. Configure CloudWatch logging for the Lambda function handling contact form submissions. 2. Log successful submissions with timestamp, source IP (for audit purposes), and field names (not sensitive values). 3. Log failed submissions and rate limit triggers. 4. Ensure logs are immutable and retained per data retention policy (minimum 1 year). 5. Implement log analysis/alerting for suspicious patterns (e.g., 10+ requests from same IP in 1 minute).

findings.title: No visible Data Processing Agreement with Plausible Analytics
findings.description: The site uses Plausible Analytics for privacy-focused website analytics. While Plausible is GDPR-compliant, GDPR Art. 28 requires a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) between the controller (Cody Jo Method/codyjo.com) and any processor (Plausible). No DPA link or evidence is visible on the site.
findings.evidence: Analytics script (line 124): `<script is:inline async src="https://analytics.codyjo.com/js/pa-KlbqzsCUHElDKXDO42D4D.js"></script>` Privacy policy references Plausible (line 16-18) with link to data policy, but does not reference a DPA or binding contract.
findings.fix: 1. Verify that you have signed Plausible's Data Processing Agreement (available on their website or via their legal terms). 2. Add statement to privacy policy: 'We have executed a Data Processing Agreement with Plausible Analytics in compliance with GDPR Article 28.' 3. Keep signed DPA in your records and make it available to data protection authorities upon request. 4. Similarly, verify DPAs exist for any other data processors (AWS, CDN providers, etc.).

findings.title: Contact form API lacks authentication and rate limiting is client-side only
findings.description: The contact form endpoint (https://ldnybm21m6.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/contact) accepts POST requests with no visible authentication (API key, OAuth, mutual TLS). Rate limiting is implemented client-side (429 error handling), but a determined attacker can bypass client-side checks by directly calling the API.
findings.evidence: Contact form submits to unauthenticated public API endpoint (line 343). Rate limiting is handled client-side (lines 402-403: 'if (res.status === 429)'). No API key, authorization header, or authentication mechanism visible in request.
findings.fix: 1. Implement server-side rate limiting using AWS WAF or API Gateway throttling (e.g., 10 requests per minute per IP). 2. Add CORS restrictions to API Gateway to limit requests to www.codyjo.com domain only. 3. Implement request signing or API key authentication if feasible (though not essential for public forms). 4. Add CAPTCHA or honeypot validation (honeypot already implemented, line 301-303, which is good). 5. Log all failed/rate-limited requests to CloudWatch.

findings.title: Contact form data transmitted over HTTPS but encryption at rest not documented
findings.description: Contact form data is transmitted to the API via HTTPS (TLS), which is correct. However, there is no visible documentation of how the contact form data is stored at rest in the backend (DynamoDB, S3, RDS, etc.) or whether it is encrypted.
findings.evidence: Contact form submits via HTTPS (line 392: `fetch(CONTACT_API, ...)`), which is secure in transit. However, backend storage method and encryption are not documented in the codebase (likely handled by AWS Lambda, but not visible).
findings.fix: 1. Verify backend storage (Lambda -> DynamoDB/S3) uses encryption at rest: enable DynamoDB encryption (default is AES-256 via AWS KMS), enable S3 default encryption. 2. Document encryption status in security documentation or compliance records. 3. If storing contact data longer-term, consider field-level encryption for sensitive data (email, message content). 4. Add statement to privacy policy: 'Contact form submissions are encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest using AWS-managed encryption.'

findings.title: Permissions-Policy header not explicitly set
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.9 - Security Headers / Best Practice
findings.description: The CSP header is comprehensive, but the Permissions-Policy (formerly Feature-Policy) header is not set. This allows all browser features (geolocation, camera, microphone) to be accessed by scripts on the page. While codyjo.com does not use these features, explicitly disabling them is a security best practice.
findings.evidence: CSP header present but no Permissions-Policy header visible. Not a critical issue since the site does not intentionally use browser features, but explicit policy strengthens security posture.
findings.fix: 1. Add Permissions-Policy header: `Permissions-Policy: geolocation=(), camera=(), microphone=(), payment=()` to explicitly disable unnecessary browser features. 2. Add to CloudFront response headers policy or Base.astro meta tag.

findings.title: Privacy policy could clarify third-party data sharing and international transfers
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions AWS hosting in the United States but does not provide Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), Adequacy Decisions, or detailed information about international transfers for EU users. GDPR Art. 44-49 require transparency and safeguards for cross-border transfers.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy line 43 states 'Infrastructure is primarily hosted in the United States' but does not explain the legal mechanism (SCCs, Adequacy Decision under Schrems II) or user rights regarding transfer. Plausible data policy link is provided (line 18) but privacy policy does not explicitly state that Plausible transfers data internationally.
findings.fix: 1. Add section to privacy policy: 'International Data Transfers: Personal data may be transferred to the United States for hosting and analytics. We rely on Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and AWS commitment to Standard Contractual Clauses for data protection.' 2. For Plausible: verify their SCCs and reference them in privacy policy. 3. Provide users the option to contact if they have concerns about transfers.

findings.title: Error messages may leak system information to users
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.26 - Logging & Monitoring Best Practice
findings.description: Contact form error messages (index.astro lines 400-407) include HTTP status codes (422, 429, 500) in user-facing messages. While these are helpful for debugging, detailed error information can leak system information to attackers. Example: 'Server error (500)' reveals that an application error occurred; 'HTTP 429' reveals rate limiting is in place.
findings.evidence: Error message line 407: `status.textContent = \`Unexpected error (${res.status}). Please try again.\`;` Shows HTTP status code to user. While helpful, it reveals system details.
findings.fix: 1. For user-facing errors: show generic message 'Something went wrong. Please try again later.' without status codes. 2. Log detailed error information (status code, stack trace) server-side for debugging. 3. Keep status codes in browser console (for developers) but not in user messages. Example: `if (res.ok) { success() } else { status.textContent = 'An error occurred. Please try again.'; console.debug('HTTP:', res.status); }`

findings.title: No self-service account deletion mechanism
findings.description: Privacy policy states users can delete accounts but settings page provides no deletion UI. Users must request deletion via email.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions deletion but no UI exists in settings page.
findings.fix: Add account deletion button to settings page with double-confirmation and cascade deletion of all user data.

findings.title: CORS headers reflect untrusted origin without validation
findings.description: Lambda API reflects incoming Origin header directly to CORS response, enabling CSRF attacks.
findings.evidence: corsHeaders() returns Origin without whitelist validation.
findings.fix: Implement origin whitelist: validate Origin against allowed domains before returning in CORS header.

findings.title: No multi-factor authentication (MFA) implementation
findings.description: Application relies solely on email and password. No TOTP, SMS, or security key support.
findings.evidence: Login only checks email/password. No MFA prompts or validation.
findings.fix: Implement TOTP-based MFA with recovery codes for account recovery.

findings.title: No explicit cookie consent banner or preference center
findings.description: Plausible Analytics is integrated but no cookie consent mechanism exists. GDPR requires opt-in consent before tracking.
findings.evidence: Analytics script included without consent banner or preference UI.
findings.fix: Add cookie consent banner disclosing essential vs. analytics cookies. Load Plausible only after user consents.

findings.title: Authentication tokens stored in localStorage without HttpOnly flag
findings.description: Bearer tokens stored in localStorage, accessible to JavaScript. XSS attacks can steal tokens.
findings.evidence: Tokens stored in localStorage: localStorage.setItem(TOKEN_KEY, token)
findings.fix: Migrate to HttpOnly, Secure cookies with SameSite=Strict. Requires backend API changes.

findings.title: Missing granular consent for Claude API / AI processing
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 13, Art. 25 (Data Protection by Design)
findings.description: Claude API integration exists but no user consent mechanism or disclosure of current usage.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions AI 'in future' but current usage and consent mechanism undefined.
findings.fix: Disclose current Claude API usage in privacy policy. Add settings toggle to opt out of AI features.

findings.title: Data export does not include all personal data
findings.description: Export only includes local app data (journals, sessions). Server-side account data, events, and attendance not included.
findings.evidence: buildLocalDataExport() only exports localStorage data, not server-side profile, events, or attendee records.
findings.fix: Implement /api/account/export endpoint retrieving all user data (profile, events, attendees, QR scans) in JSON/CSV.

findings.title: No documented session timeout or inactivity lockout
findings.description: Sessions remain active indefinitely until manual logout. No inactivity timeout.
findings.evidence: logout() clears token but no automatic timeout on inactivity.
findings.fix: Implement client-side activity tracking with 30-minute inactivity timeout and warning.

findings.title: No documented Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)
findings.description: Processing personal data of event organizers, attendees, speakers without documented risk assessment.
findings.evidence: No DPIA document found. Privacy policy lacks formal risk assessment.
findings.fix: Conduct and document DPIA covering: purpose, data categories, recipients, risks, and mitigation measures.

findings.title: No age verification or minor data protections
findings.description: Privacy policy states 16+ but no age gate. Organizers can add children as attendees without age verification.
findings.evidence: No age verification in registration. No parental consent mechanism for minor attendees.
findings.fix: Add optional 'creating events for minors' flag. Implement privacy-by-default for child-focused events.

findings.title: No visible client-side error/audit logging disclosure
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions logs but no detailed retention policy or user audit log access.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy vaguely mentions 'operational logs ~30 days' without detail.
findings.fix: Document logging policy in privacy statement. Provide API/UI for users to request audit logs.

findings.title: Privacy policy lacks detail on event data retention
findings.description: Retention policy for guest lists, QR scans, gallery uploads is unclear.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states 'data remains until deletion' but event-specific retention undefined.
findings.fix: Update privacy policy with event data retention schedule (e.g., deleted when event deleted).

findings.title: Password hashing algorithm not disclosed
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions 'hashed password' but does not specify algorithm (bcrypt, argon2, etc.).
findings.evidence: Privacy policy: 'hashed password' with no algorithm specified.
findings.fix: Disclose password hashing algorithm in privacy policy or security page.

findings.title: Privacy policy does not mention Anthropic DPA
findings.description: Claude API integration not fully disclosed. No mention of Anthropic as data processor or DPA.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy mentions AI but not Anthropic by name or DPA status.
findings.fix: Update privacy policy to disclose: 'AI processing via Anthropic Claude API, bound by DPA.' Verify DPA exists.

findings.title: Encryption method for backups not disclosed
findings.description: Encryption of backed-up journal entries uses AES-256-GCM but method not disclosed to users.
findings.evidence: Code uses AES-256-GCM with PBKDF2 600k iterations but privacy policy doesn't mention.
findings.fix: Add note: 'Backups encrypted with AES-256-GCM; key derived via PBKDF2.'

findings.title: No mechanism to object to marketing/profiling
findings.description: Privacy policy mentions 'right to object' but no unsubscribe UI or marketing preference controls.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy claims right to object. No settings UI for marketing opt-out.
findings.fix: Add 'Communication Preferences' to settings with toggle for marketing emails.

findings.title: Account Deletion API Endpoint Not Exposed in UI
findings.description: The API endpoint /api/account (DELETE) implements the GDPR right to erasure by deleting all user data, but the UI in settings/page.tsx does not expose a 'Delete Account' button. Users cannot exercise their right to erasure without direct API access, violating GDPR Art. 17 requirement that erasure requests must be possible through the service interface.
findings.evidence: The settings page exports/import handleExport and handleDeleteWeight but does not implement handleDeleteAccount. The API handler at lambda/api/index.mjs:2095 fully implements deletion.
findings.fix: Add a 'Delete Account' section in the Settings page with password confirmation dialog, similar to the Export Data button. The confirmation should clearly explain this is irreversible. Implement handleDeleteAccount in the settings page that calls POST /api/account with the user's password.

findings.title: Missing Data Retention Policy Disclosure
findings.description: The privacy policy discloses what data is collected and how it's stored, but does not explicitly state how long data is retained. Article states 'Your data is retained for as long as your account exists' but provides no guidance on automated deletion, archival, or regulatory retention timelines. This violates GDPR Art. 13(2)(a) requirement to inform users of retention periods.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 'Data Retention & Deletion' (lines 165-177) states data is retained indefinitely during account lifetime, but does not specify if/when deleted data is purged from backups, archives, or logs.
findings.fix: Expand the privacy policy to explicitly state: (1) retention period for each data category (e.g., 'Nutrition logs retained for account lifetime, deleted immediately on account deletion'), (2) backup and archive retention periods (e.g., 'Backups retained for 30 days after deletion'), (3) logs retention policy (e.g., 'API logs retained 30 days, CloudFront logs 90 days'), (4) any regulatory retention requirements if applicable.

findings.title: No Data Protection Officer Contact Information
findings.description: The privacy policy provides a contact email (noreply@codyjo.com) for privacy inquiries but does not identify whether a Data Protection Officer (DPO) exists, and if so, does not provide their direct contact. GDPR Art. 13(2)(b) and Art. 37 require organizations subject to mandatory DPO requirements to publish DPO contact details.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy lists only general support email (noreply@codyjo.com). No reference to a DPO or privacy team contact.
findings.fix: Clarify in the privacy policy whether Cody Jo Method has appointed a Data Protection Officer. If yes, provide DPO email/contact. If no, add a statement: 'We have not appointed a dedicated DPO as we do not meet GDPR DPO appointment triggers. For privacy matters, contact privacy@fuel.codyjo.com.' Ensure the designated privacy contact is monitored and responsive.

findings.title: No Right to Object to Direct Marketing (if applicable)
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 6 & 21 (Lawful Basis & Right to Object)
findings.description: The privacy policy does not mention direct marketing, newsletter subscriptions, or a mechanism for users to object to direct marketing processing. If email marketing is planned, there must be an explicit right to object per GDPR Art. 21(2).
findings.evidence: Privacy policy does not mention email marketing, promotional communications, or unsubscribe mechanisms. Resend integration is mentioned for transactional emails only (password reset, verification), but no mention of marketing email capability.
findings.fix: If email marketing is not planned, no action needed. If marketing is planned: (1) Update privacy policy to disclose marketing email processing and lawful basis (explicit opt-in consent), (2) Implement preference center allowing users to unsubscribe from marketing, (3) Ensure all marketing emails include unsubscribe link per CAN-SPAM/GDPR.

findings.title: No Documented Session Timeout or Inactivity Limits
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.1 & A.9.4 (User Access Management & Session Management)
findings.description: JWT tokens are issued with 30-day expiry, but there is no documented session timeout for inactivity. Users accessing sensitive health data could remain authenticated indefinitely without activity, increasing exposure if a device is lost or stolen. ISO 27001 A.9.4.3 requires session timeout controls.
findings.evidence: JWT creation at line 162 shows 30-day absolute expiry (86400 * 30), but no idle/inactivity timeout mechanism. Frontend does not show signs of logout on inactivity.
findings.fix: Implement idle session timeout: (1) On frontend, track user activity (mouse, keyboard, touch), (2) If inactive for 15-30 minutes, prompt user to confirm activity or auto-logout, (3) Send reminder notification before logout, (4) Clear tokens and cached data on logout. Document the policy in security standards.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) Documentation
findings.description: The system processes sensitive health data including medication records, body composition, and wellness information. GDPR Art. 35 requires a DPIA for processing that is likely to result in high risk, particularly systematic monitoring or large-scale processing. No evidence of DPIA documentation.
findings.evidence: README and CLAUDE.md do not reference any DPIA process, documentation, or risk assessment. The system processes sensitive health/medical data, which typically triggers DPIA requirements.
findings.fix: Conduct a DPIA documenting: (1) Purpose and necessity of health data processing, (2) Risk assessment for confidentiality/integrity breaches, (3) Mitigation measures (encryption, access controls, retention limits), (4) Residual risk assessment, (5) Consultation with supervisory authority if high risk remains. Document and retain for audit purposes.

findings.title: API Keys Stored in Lambda Environment Variables
findings.description: The Anthropic API key and USDA API key are stored in Lambda environment variables (README.md:351), not in AWS Secrets Manager. While Secrets Manager is used for JWT_SECRET, external API keys should also use Secrets Manager for rotation and audit trail benefits.
findings.evidence: Environment variables documented: 'ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: Lambda env' and 'USDA_API_KEY: Lambda env'. These keys are visible in Lambda console and CloudWatch logs if logging is verbose.
findings.fix: Move external API keys to Secrets Manager: (1) Store ANTHROPIC_API_KEY and USDA_API_KEY in Secrets Manager (not env vars), (2) Update Lambda to fetch from Secrets Manager like JWT_SECRET, (3) Implement key rotation policy with Secrets Manager automatic rotation, (4) Audit key access via CloudTrail.

findings.title: Limited Audit Trail for Data Access/Modification
findings.description: API logs (30-day retention) capture requests but the privacy policy and code do not document comprehensive audit trails for sensitive data access (e.g., who accessed whose medication records, when wellness data was exported). ISO 27001 A.12.4 requires audit trails for security-relevant events.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy states 'API request logs are retained for 30 days and include request timestamps, HTTP methods, and response status codes' but does not mention logging of user IDs, data categories accessed, or modification timestamps for individual records.
findings.fix: Enhance audit logging: (1) Log all data access with user ID, endpoint, data category (food/medication/wellness), and result code, (2) Log all data modifications (PUT/DELETE) with old and new values (or hashes for large fields), (3) Retain logs for minimum 1 year, (4) Implement CloudWatch log insights queries for data access audits, (5) Document in privacy policy: 'Data access is logged for security and compliance purposes and retained for 1 year.'

findings.title: Password Requirement Discrepancy Between Backend and Frontend
findings.description: The backend requires passwords to be at least 10 characters (lambda/api/index.mjs:422), but the frontend validation message says 'At least 8 characters' (register/page.tsx:118). This discrepancy could confuse users and create inconsistent UX.
findings.evidence: Frontend placeholder text: 'At least 8 characters' but backend check at lambda/api/index.mjs:422 requires: 'Password must be at least 10 characters'.
findings.fix: Update frontend validation message to match backend requirement. Change register/page.tsx:118 placeholder and error messages from 'At least 8 characters' to 'At least 10 characters'. Verify login page doesn't have similar discrepancies.

findings.title: Missing Cookie Consent Banner
findings.description: No explicit cookie consent implementation. While Plausible is cookieless by design, GDPR best practice requires users to opt-in to analytics before tracking begins. The absence of a consent banner may violate GDPR Article 7 (conditions for consent) and Article 13 (information to be provided).
findings.fix: Implement cookie consent banner (e.g., CookieBot, OneTrust, or custom) that: (1) obtains explicit opt-in before Plausible tracking; (2) discloses third-party cookies; (3) provides easy opt-out; (4) respects Do Not Track header.

findings.title: No Explicit Lawful Basis for Account Activity Tracking
findings.description: Privacy policy discloses tracking (Plausible, Anthropic API calls) but does not explicitly state which lawful basis applies to activity monitoring. GDPR requires one of six bases (consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interest) for each processing activity. Current language is vague on whether tracking is contractual, consent-based, or legitimate interest.
findings.fix: Update privacy policy to explicitly state: '(1) Plausible analytics relies on legitimate interest (app improvement); (2) API call logging relies on contract performance; (3) Account activity tracking for fraud prevention relies on legitimate interest.' Include legitimacy assessment balancing.

findings.title: Missing DPIA Documentation Reference
findings.description: No evidence of Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) documentation for high-risk processing activities: (1) E2E encryption key storage and journal encryption; (2) Third-party API integrations (Anthropic, Bolls.life, Resend); (3) User behavior tracking (Plausible); (4) Cross-border data transfers. GDPR requires DPIA for processing using new technologies or large-scale processing.
findings.fix: Conduct and document DPIA covering: (1) Purpose and necessity of tracking activities; (2) Risk assessment for E2E encryption implementation; (3) Third-party processor agreements and risk analysis; (4) Mitigation measures (e.g., data minimization, access controls). Store DPIA document in compliant location and update privacy policy to reference it.

findings.title: Account Deletion Does Not Cover Third-Party Data Retention
findings.description: User account deletion removes local data (studies, journals, bookmarks) but privacy policy does not clearly explain retention in third-party systems: (1) Resend email logs (sending confirmation/password reset emails) may retain data for compliance/spam prevention; (2) Anthropic API call history retained for 30 days; (3) Bolls.life does not delete user query history. Incomplete data erasure violates GDPR Article 17.
findings.fix: Update privacy policy with: (1) 'Resend email logs are retained for X days for spam/abuse prevention; we request deletion but cannot guarantee immediate removal'; (2) 'Anthropic API call history is retained for 30 days per their terms; we do not control deletion'; (3) 'Bolls.life does not delete query history.' Add account deletion endpoint response that acknowledges third-party retention periods. Consider adding Data Processing Agreement (DPA) with Anthropic and Resend to ensure right to erasure.

findings.title: No API Export for Synced Cloud Data
findings.description: App offers local data export (via IndexedDB) but no comprehensive API endpoint to export all user data in structured format. Right to data portability requires users to obtain a copy of personal data they've provided in a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format (e.g., CSV, JSON). Missing export for: studies, journals, bookmarks, reading history from DynamoDB.
findings.fix: Implement GET /api/data-export endpoint that: (1) Returns ZIP containing JSON exports of studies, journals, bookmarks, reading history; (2) Includes metadata (creation date, modification date, encryption status); (3) Sends via email or direct download; (4) Logs data portability request for audit trail. Add UI button in account settings to trigger export.

findings.title: No Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) Support
findings.description: App uses password-only authentication. No MFA option (TOTP, SMS, email confirmation on login) for accounts containing sensitive user data (journals, personal Bible study notes). Single factor authentication is insufficient for ISO 27001 baseline and NIST guidelines for high-value targets.
findings.fix: Implement optional MFA: (1) TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) via Google Authenticator/Authy - lowest friction; (2) Email confirmation on new device login (implicit MFA); (3) SMS OTP (optional, requires SMS provider integration). Start with TOTP (small effort), add email MFA next (medium effort).

findings.title: No Rate Limiting on Non-Auth API Endpoints
findings.description: Rate limiting is implemented for auth endpoints (registration 3/hr, login 5/15min) but not for general API endpoints (journals, studies, bookmarks). Missing rate limits on: GET /api/journals, POST /journals, DELETE /journals, GET /studies, POST /studies. Attackers could enumerate resources or cause DoS without detection.
findings.fix: Implement global API rate limiting: (1) Per-user limit: 100 requests/minute for authenticated users; (2) Per-IP limit: 1000 requests/minute for all traffic; (3) Endpoint-specific limits for expensive operations (e.g., journal encryption: 10/minute). Use middleware (rate-limiter-flexible, express-rate-limit) and return 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header.

findings.title: No Documented CORS Configuration or Origin Whitelist
findings.description: Backend sends CORS headers (Access-Control-Allow-Origin) but no documented approach to validate origins. No visible code enforcing strict origin whitelist. Could permit unauthorized cross-origin requests if misconfigured.
findings.fix: Implement explicit CORS whitelist: (1) Define approved origins in environment variable: CORS_ORIGINS=https://selahscripture.com,https://app.selahscripture.com; (2) Validate Origin header against whitelist on every request; (3) Return 403 Forbidden for non-whitelisted origins; (4) Document in README. Use express-cors with credentials: true and origin function.

findings.title: No Documented Log Encryption or Access Controls
findings.description: CloudWatch logs contain sensitive information (auth events, account deletion, encryption key setup) but no evidence of: (1) log encryption at rest; (2) log access controls (IAM policies); (3) retention policies; (4) log integrity verification. AWS Lambda logs are readable by any AWS account user with CloudWatch permissions.
findings.fix: Implement log security: (1) Enable CloudWatch log encryption (AWS KMS key); (2) Restrict CloudWatch access via IAM policy (only security team); (3) Set retention policy to 90 days (NIST baseline); (4) Archive logs to S3 (immutable) for long-term storage; (5) Enable S3 Object Lock for tamper-proof storage. Document in security runbook.

findings.title: No Documented Incident Response Runbook or Breach Notification Procedure
findings.description: App handles sensitive user data (journals, personal notes) but no documented incident response plan. Missing: (1) breach detection procedures; (2) containment steps; (3) notification timeline (GDPR requires 72-hour notification to DPA); (4) communication templates for users; (5) forensics/investigation procedures. Critical for compliance with GDPR Article 33 and ISO 27001 A.16.
findings.fix: Create and document incident response runbook: (1) Detection & triage (monitor CloudWatch for suspicious activity); (2) Containment (revoke sessions, isolate affected accounts); (3) Forensics (preserve logs, identify scope); (4) Internal notification (CEO, legal, security team within 24h); (5) User notification (breach notification email template); (6) DPA notification (prepare GDPR breach notification); (7) Post-incident review (RCA). Store runbook in shared secure location and conduct tabletop exercise quarterly.

findings.title: API Request Size Limit Not Uniformly Enforced
findings.description: Journal endpoint returns 413 for payloads >200KB, but other endpoints lack explicit size limits. Missing limits could allow: (1) large payload DoS attacks; (2) memory exhaustion; (3) database overload. Should enforce consistently across all endpoints accepting user input.
findings.fix: Implement global request size limit middleware: (1) Limit all JSON payloads to 100KB (express.json({limit: '100kb'})); (2) Limit file uploads to 10MB; (3) Document limits in API spec. Customize per-endpoint if needed (e.g., journal encryption data may need higher limit).

findings.title: Age Verification Relies on Self-Attestation Only
findings.regulation: COPPA, UK Online Safety Act, California Age-Appropriate Design Code
findings.description: Registration form includes checkbox 'I confirm I am at least 16 years old' but no technical verification. Self-attestation is easily bypassed by users under 16. No parental consent flow for minors 13-15. No age-gating documented in privacy policy beyond text disclosure. Cannot verify actual user age.
